blocks.statement: I can apply U = −GMm/r to compute the PE of an asteroid at different distances and compute ΔU as it falls (= KE gained).

blocks.whatWeKnow: 2026-XJ's PE depends on its distance from Earth. As it gets closer, U becomes MORE NEGATIVE — meaning the asteroid is GAINING kinetic energy.
blocks.connection: ΔU as the asteroid falls is the KE it brings to impact. Day 19 puts a number on this.

blocks.markdown:
## The universal formula

**U = − G·M·m / r** — the gravitational PE between two masses M and m separated by distance r (center to center).

The conventions:

- **PE = 0 at infinity** — the natural zero, since gravity fades to nothing out there.
- **PE is negative everywhere closer** — the object sits in a gravitational *well*. You'd have to do positive work to lift it out to infinity.
- **Falling in: U gets MORE negative** — and since total energy is conserved, KE goes UP by exactly the amount U went down. ΔU is the KE the fall delivers.

No constant-g assumption anywhere. This formula is honest at any altitude — desk height or Moon distance — which is why it's the one the asteroid math needs.

terms.definition: The universal gravitational PE between two masses M and m separated by distance r. PE = 0 at infinity; negative everywhere closer. A 1-kg object at Earth's surface (r = 6.37×10⁶ m): U = −(6.67e-11)(6e24)(1)/(6.37e6) ≈ −6.28 × 10⁷ J.
terms.cognate: Sp. energía potencial gravitacional universal · Pt. energia potencial gravitacional universal

blocks.title: Negative U doesn't mean "missing energy"
blocks.markdown: It means the object is in a gravitational well — you'd have to do work to lift it out to infinity.

blocks.prompt: PE OF AN ASTEROID AT THREE DISTANCES — A 1000-kg test mass at (a) 384,000 km from Earth's center, (b) Earth's surface (6,371 km), (c) at infinity. Find U at each, then ΔU from (a) to (b).
blocks.answer: The 1000-kg mass GAINS 6.15 × 10¹⁰ J of KE falling from Moon's distance to surface.

blocks.title: Two derivations, one number
blocks.markdown: A 500-kg meteorite from infinity hits Earth at v ≈ 11.2 km/s — Earth's escape velocity. Same number, two derivations. Day 20 confirms.

blocks.prompt: KE GAINED FROM INFINITY — A 500-kg meteorite falls from infinity to Earth's surface (no air friction). How much KE does it gain?
blocks.givenHint: m = 500 kg · r_start = ∞ (so U_start = 0) · r_end = 6.37 × 10⁶ m · G = 6.67×10⁻¹¹ · M = 5.97 × 10²⁴ kg

blocks.prompt: A 500-kg object falls from infinity to Earth's surface (no air drag). What KE does it gain? Then complete the frame.
blocks.frame: An asteroid GAINS kinetic energy as it falls because its U becomes ___ (more negative). The total ME stays constant, so ___ goes up by the same amount.
